comments.text: Let$$ B:=\sup_f \ \liminf_{r\to\infty}\frac{\max_n |a_n|\,r^n}{\max_{|z|=r}|f(z)|}. $$1. In addition to the upper bound $B<2/\pi$, [ClHa64] also proved the lower bound $B>4/7$. 2. This problem is also listed in [HaLi18] (see arxiv link, springer link), where it is recorded that the best known bounds are$$ 0.57143\approx \frac{4}{7} < B < \frac{2}{\pi}\approx 0.63662. $$3. My friend He and I have just posted a paper (arXiv:2602.12217) in which we generalize the function construction from [ClHa64] and obtain a stronger explicit lower bound$$B>0.58507.$$This work made exploratory use of ChatGPT-5.2 Pro, with all mathematical arguments subsequently checked by the human authors. Reference. [HaLi18] W. K. Hayman, E. F. Lingham, Research Problems in Function Theory: Fiftieth Anniversary Edition, Problem Books in Mathematics, Springer, Cham, 2019. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
I made a page for the constant $B$ in the optimization constant repository.

I have run GPT in a near-autonomous process to try to improve the constant. Here is the writeup. We did not really succeed. To summarize, the bound $B > 0.5850724$ in He-Tang is very slightly improved to $B > 0.5850788$ (both numbers rounded down to nearest). More ambitious improvements were attempted but they did not succeed. I was debating with myself whether this should count as an improvement. But seeing the AlphaEvolve's precedents in [36] (also recorded here) and [1097] which are of similar scales, I believe it should count. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Clunie and Hayman [ClHa64] claim a better upper bound of $2/\pi-c$ for some explicit constant $c>0$, which they do not work out but could be done in principle. Is there a reason you do not cite this improved bound in your comment/paper; do you have a reason to doubt this proof?
FWIW, one probably intends for $f$ to be transcendental; otherwise, e.g. $f(z)=z+1$ gives a limit of $1$. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

It looks good to me. I looked at [ClHa64], and on the first page they already state that they assume \(f(z)\) is not a polynomial. Therefore, in the statement of the problem, “entire function” should be replaced by “transcendental entire function.” (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
